w: A knee-high bronze terrier on a granite drinking fountain, and Edinburgh's smallest listed building. William Brodie sculpted Bobby from life in 1873, paid for by Baroness Burdett-Coutts; the story goes the dog guarded his master's grave in the kirkyard for 14 years. Tourists rub his nose gold, against the council's wishes.

Fifteen feet of iron staves bound in hoops, nearly seven tonnes, with a 20-inch bore — a siege bombard built in Mons in 1449 and handed to James II in 1454. She could throw a 330-pound stone ball; her own barrel burst firing a salute in 1680. Sir Walter Scott campaigned to haul her back from the Tower of London.

A heart picked out in granite setts in the Royal Mile cobbles, and Edinburghers still spit on it. It marks the doorway of the Old Tolbooth — town hall, prison and public gallows until it came down in 1817. The spit began as contempt for the jail; now it's often for luck. Walter Scott gave it the name in 1818.

A red sandstone monolith nearly three metres tall, reputedly older than 5,000 years, now wedged into a recess in a suburban garden wall in Fairmilehead. Six weathered cup-marks pock one face; the hill around it once held cairns, cists and burial urns. Cat Stane, Kel Stane, Camus Stone — it has answered to all of them.

More accused witches were strangled and burned here, on the Castle Esplanade, than anywhere else in Scotland. Patrick Geddes raised this cast-iron fountain in 1894, designed by John Duncan: a serpent coiled round two healing gods, a foxglove — evil and wisdom in one emblem. The dates 1479 and 1722 bracket the terror.

Six iron casts of Antony Gormley's own body trail down the Water of Leith from the modern-art gallery to the docks at Leith, each facing a different way — Horizon, Ground, Left, Right, Sky. Four stand in the river itself, doubling as flood gauges as the water climbs over them. Installed in 2010, and promptly toppled by a flood.

A single 1645 plague tomb, carved with a grinning skull, marooned in a tenement back garden off Chamberlain Road. John Livingston, an apothecary, died in that year's outbreak and was buried out on the open Burgh Muir rather than any churchyard. The Latin cut into the stone: 'Mors patet; Hora latet' — death is sure, the hour obscure.

A bronze cannon on Calton Hill that crossed three empires to get here. Cast in Iberia under Spanish arms, sent to serve the Portuguese Indies from Mozambique to Macao, then passed to the kings of Arakan and hauled to Mandalay in 1785. Britain seized it when Upper Burma fell in 1885, and shipped it north the next year.
